title: The Dynamic Properties of Financial-Market Equilibrium with Trading Fees
abstract: We incorporate trading fees into a dynamic, multiagent general-equilibrium model in which traders optimally decide when to trade. For that purpose, we propose an innovative algorithm that synchronizes the traders. Securities prices are not so much affected by the payment of the fees itself, but rather by the trade-off that the traders face between smoothing consumption and smoothing holdings. In calibrated examples, the interest rate and welfare decline with trading fees, while risk premia and volatilities increase. Liquidity risk and expected liquidity are priced, leading to deviations from the consumption-CAPM. With trading fees, capital is slow-moving, generating slow price reversal.

article.introduction:
The financial sector of the economyissues and trades securities. But, more importantly, it provides a service to clients, namely access to the financial market and trading. This service is provided for a fee. Our objective in this paper is to shed light on the impact of these trading fees on trading strategies, asset prices, return moments, and liquidity premia—in a general-equilibrium economy.
In particular, we first seek to identify the economic mechanisms through which endogenous (il)liquidity, arising from trading fees, influences securities prices, their returns, and their volatilities. We next seek to understand how liquidity and liquidity risk are priced in equilibrium and how these premia fluctuate over time. Finally, we wish to determine whether the slow movement of investment capital that arises naturally in the presence of trading fees can explain a slow reversal of the corresponding securities prices.
To address the above questions, we incorporate trading fees into a dynamic general-equilibrium economy in which traders optimally and endogenously decide when and how much to trade. Traders are endowed with an every-period motive for trading, over and above the long-term need to trade for lifetime planning. That is, they receive endowments that are not fully hedgeable because, even without trading fees, the financial market is incomplete. Hence, whenever a trader's realized endowment differs from the amount he has hedged, he has a desire to adjust his portfolio positions. However, transactions are subject to trading fees.
As a way of constructing a simple model, we bypass intermediaries and their pricing policy, and instead let traders serve as dealers for, and pay trading fees to, each other.1We take the fee function as given, but choose the functional form in such a way that it reflects one special and important feature of the cost of financial services: financial-market traders sometimes buy and at other times sell the very same security, paying a positive fee irrespective of the direction of the trade. Specifically, we assume that, to an approximation, the trading fee is proportional to the value of the shares traded, and thus introduces a kink at zero in the trading-fee function.
As a result, liquidity in our model is endogenous. Within a so-called “no-trade region,” it is optimal for a trader not to trade, thereby preventing other traders from trading with him. A trader's reluctance to trade creates a negative (pecuniary) externality for other traders, and, as a result, investment capital in the economy is slow-moving. This is an additional endogenous, stochastic, and quantitatively important consequence of the trading fee. Illiquidity begets illiquidity. Hence, when purchasing a security, a trader must anticipate his, and other people's, desire and ability to resell the security in the marketplace at a later time.
We first demonstrate the impact of this endogenous process of (il)liquidity on equilibrium prices. We show analytically that differences between equilibrium securities prices in the presence of trading fees and those in the absence of trading fees result from changes in the state prices and the consumption of individual agents. In other words, the differences do not result so much from the payment of the trading fees itself as they do from a trade-off that the traders face between smoothing consumption and smoothing holdings (to reduce the cost of trading).
Second, we provide quantitative results for an illustrative setting with two traded securities: (i) a fully liquid bond and (ii) a stock that is subject to trading fees. We document that the resulting higher consumption growth volatility of the individual traders pushes both securities' prices up—due to a precautionary-savings effect. As a result, even the stock price is increasing in the level of its fees. Moreover, the higher consumption growth volatility has a considerably negative impact on traders' welfare. The same economic mechanism affects rates of return. While the rate of interest is reduced due to the precautionary-savings effect, the equity premium, the stock's return volatility, and its Sharpe ratio increase.
Next, we show how, in the presence of trading fees and endogenous trading decisions, various liquidity (risk) premia arise, relative to the classic consumption-CAPM (CCAPM). In particular, in our economy, not only liquidity risk but also expected future liquidity generate a premium over and above consumption risk. We next document the variation of each term over time. While the unconditional average value of the CCAPM deviation is due mostly to the liquidity risk premium, its unconditional variance is driven mostly by fluctuations of the conditionally expected liquidity term.
In an extension of our basic model to the case of three traders, we show that deviations from the frictionless equilibrium are not reduced when more traders are present in the market. For example, the welfare loss and the waiting time between trades increase relative to the two-trader economy. Intuitively, while more traders in a market implies more people potentially ready to trade, it also implies more people who need to trade (for partially idiosyncratic reasons). Interestingly, a sizeable fraction of trades in the stock market take place between two traders only (bilateral trade).
Finally, we document that the slow movement of investment capital that arises naturally in our model because of traders' reluctance to trade can explain slow price reversal. In the model with trading fees, when a shock occurs, traders (partially) adjust immediately to that impulse, but they also react later on; that is due to hysteresis. Indeed, the impulse moves a fee-paying trader closer to a trade boundary, so that when subsequent shocks arrive in the same direction, he will act to a further extent than he would have acted in the absence of the earlier impulse. As a result, when a shock occurs, the price of a security (over)reacts, while quantities do not adjust completely at first. When quantities do adjust later, the price movement is reversed, leading to a pattern that is known as “slow price reversal.”
The paper also makes a significant methodological contribution to the solution of equilibrium models with trading fees. We define a form of Walrasian equilibrium that is the limit of a sequence of equilibria in which each trader's complementary slackness condition (resulting, as explained below, from the trading fees) has been relaxed, and we develop an algorithm that takes that limit. This algorithm delivers an exact numerical equilibrium that synchronizes like clockwork the traders in the implementation of their trades, which allows us to analyze both the way in which trades take place and the way in which prices are formed and evolve. He and Modest (1995), Jouini and Kallal (1995), and Luttmer (1996) establish bounds on asset prices, but we reach a full description.
Many predictions from our model are consistent with empirical evidence. For example, prior research documents that less liquid stocks earn higher returns and are more volatile (see SectionV.Afor a discussion of the empirical literature). Also, we make new empirical predictions about, for instance, the relation between trading fees and the speed of price reversal. These predictions are empirically refutable, as we verify quantitatively that the bid-ask midpoint can be used as a proxy to study the predictions of our model. We also provide guidance for future empirical research on endogenous liquidity (risk) premia in unconditional and conditional asset pricing models.
Trading fees are not negligible, as documented by two recent papers. First, French (2008) estimates the “cost of active investing” (defined as the difference between the spreads, fees, as well as other trading costs paid by an active investor, and the costs for holding the market portfolio) to be 0.67% per year of the aggregate market value—in spite of increasing competition and technological advances. This estimate can be interpreted as a lower bound on the actual costs of spreads and fees.2Second, Novy-Marx and Velikov (2016), who estimate the cost of implementing investment strategies based on “pricing anomalies,” conclude that most asset pricing anomalies could not be exploited profitably if trading costs were to be paid. That is, trading costs make room for, or allow, the anomalies to continue to exist.3
Our paper is related to existing studies of portfolio choice under transactions costs such as Magill and Constantinides (1976), Constantinides (1976a,b,1986), Davis and Norman (1990), Dumas and Luciano (1991), Edirisinghe, Naik, and Uppal (1993), Gennotte and Jung (1994), Shreve and Soner (1994), Cvitanić and Karatzas (1996), Leland (2000), Longstaff (2001), Bouchard (2002), Obizhaeva and Wang (2013), Liu and Loewenstein (2002), Jang et al. (2007), Gerhold et al. (2014), and Gârleanu and Pedersen (2013), among others. As Dumas and Luciano (1991) note, many of these papers suffer from a logical quasi-inconsistency. Not only do they assume an exogenous process for securities' returns, as do all portfolio optimization papers, but they do so in a way that is incompatible with the portfolio policy that is produced by the optimization. That is, when transactions costs are linear and a “no-trade region” arises, they nevertheless assume that trades remain available in the marketplace.4Obviously, the assumption must be somehow made that some traders, other than the one whose portfolio is being optimized, do not incur transactions costs. In the present paper, all traders (except in SectionV.C) face a trading fee.
The papers by Heaton and Lucas (1996), Vayanos (1998), Vayanos and Vila (1999), and Lo, Mamaysky, and Wang (2004) study the impact of transactions costs in equilibrium and are direct ancestors of the present paper.5Heaton and Lucas (1996) derive a stationary equilibrium, but in the neighborhood of zero trade the cost is assumed to be quadratic, so that traders trade all the time in small quantities. Hence, equilibrium behavior is qualitatively different from that we produce. In Vayanos (1998) and Vayanos and Vila (1999), a trader's only motive to trade is his finite lifetime: when young, he buys securities that he can resell during his old age. Here, we introduce a motive to trade that operates at each point in time. Lo, Mamaysky, and Wang (2004) study fixed costs in a model with exponential utility. Individual traders' endowments provide the motive to trade, but the amount of aggregate resources available is nonstochastic. In contrast, in our paper fees are proportional, preferences can be specified at will (although we present illustrative results for time-additive utility only), and aggregate and individual resources are free to follow arbitrary stochastic processes. To our knowledge, ours is the first paper that obtains such an equilibrium.6
As far as the solution method is concerned, our analysis is closely related, in ways we explain below, to the “dual method” proposed by Bismut (1973) and Cvitanić and Karatzas (1992) and used by Jouini and Kallal (1995), Cvitanić and Karatzas (1996), Cuoco (1997), Deelstra, Nizar, and Touzi (2001), and Kallsen and Muhle-Karbe (2010), among others.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIintroduces the model. SectionIIdiscusses the equilibrium without trading fees, focusing in particular on the motive to trade. In SectionIII, we add trading fees, define equilibrium, and analytically derive bounds on securities prices. In SectionIV, we use a numerical illustration to quantify the impact of trading fees on trading decisions, consumption, asset prices, and rates of return. Finally, in SectionVwe develop three applications: (i) a CCAPM in the presence of trading fees and endogenous trading, (ii) an extension to three traders, and (iii) price reversals produced by the model. SectionVIconcludes. Technical details are relegated to the Appendices.
article.I. A Model of a Financial Market with Fr:
We work with a generic model of a dynamic exchange economy. Time is discrete and the horizon is finite, with time being indexed byt=0,…,T. There exists a single consumption good, which is also the numéraire. Exogenous uncertainty is described by a tree or lattice. A given node of the tree at timetis followed byKtnodes at timet+1with transition probabilities{πt,t+1,j}j=1Kt.7The economy is populated withLtradersl=1,…,L, who receive individual endowments that are adapted to the nodes of the tree or lattice. They can trade multiple financial assets. However, financial markets are incomplete.
We model a competitive financial market withItraded securities:i=1,..,I. Securities are described by their payoffs{δt,i}, which are exogenous and placed on the tree or lattice. Some securities can be short-lived, making a single payoff and being reissued immediately—for example, a risk-free, one-period bond. Other securities can be long-lived, making payoffs at all datest. The total supply of securityiis constant and denoted byθ¯i, which allows for securities in zero but also positive net supply. Financial markets are assumed to beincomplete. That is, at each node of the tree, there exist fewer nonredundant securities than future nodes.
Financial-market transactions can entail trading fees, with the fees calculated on the basis of the transaction price. When a trader sells one unit of securityiat timet, he receives, in units of consumption good, the transaction price multiplied by1−εi,t(0≤εi,t<1); when he buys one unit, he must pay the transaction price times1+λi,t(0≤λi,t). In all illustrations, we assume that these fee rates are constant, but more generally they must be adapted to the nodes of the tree or lattice. We assume that all fees are paid to a central pot, with the fees being redistributed in the form of transfers. The transfers are taken by a trader to be lump-sum (but recurring) amounts that enter his budget constraint but do not generate an additional term in his first-order conditions. In this way, the trader remains purely competitive in that he only takes into account the cost of his own actions, not the benefits he may receive from the actions of other traders.8
We consider a recursive Walrasian market for the securities.9The auctioneer calls out time-tprices, which we refer to as “posted” prices and denote by{St,i;i=1,…,I;t=0,…,T}. Assuming that all markets after timetclear, traders then submit flow quantity schedules, knowing that fees will be calculated on the basis of the posted price in the case of a transaction. If the flow demanded is positive at the price that is called out, the trader intends to buy; otherwise he intends to sell. The auctioneer determines the price at which markets clear, with one possible outcome of the clearing being zero trade. As a result, the posted price of a security is an effective transaction price only when a transaction takes place, but it is always posted by the Walrasian auctioneer.
Each traderlis endowed withθ¯l,ishares of securityiand a stream of exogenous individual endowments{el,t∈R++;l=1,…,L;t=0,…,T}.10For traderl, we denote bycl,this consumption at timet, and byθl,t,ithe number of units of securityiin his hands after all transactions at timet, so that{cl,t;t=0,…,T}and{θl,t,i;t=0,…,T;i=1,…,I}describe his consumption and investment policies. He chooses consumption and investment policies to maximize expected utility over his lifetime consumption.
While we write the utility function1in the additive form, given the recursive technique to be used, it would be easy to handle recursive utility, especially in the isoelastic case. Also, the utility function may contain arguments other than a trader's time-tconsumption (hence the · as an argument), for example, past consumption in the case of habit formation.
Throughout the paper, we use a specific setting to illustrate the predictions of our model. Here, we quickly introduce this setting, which we describe in more detail in AppendixA.
We consider an economy with two traded securities: a short-lived riskless security (the “bond” ),i=1, that is in zero net supply and is not subject to trading fees, and a long-lived claim (the “stock” ),i=2, that is in unit net supply and pays out dividendδt,2. Trading the stock entails trading fees, whereε=λis assumed to be constant. We first consider the case of two traders. All traders have the same preferences of the external-habit type, implemented as surplus consumption, similar to Campbell and Cochrane (1999).12
Aggregate output is assumed to follow a binomial tree, with the expected growth rate and its volatility matching their empirical counterparts. Dividends on the stock are simply modeled as a constant fraction of aggregate output. The remainder is distributed as endowments, with the endowment shares of the traders following an independent, two-state Markov chain. Accordingly, each trader facesKt+1=2×2=4states of nature for the immediate future: positive versus negative growth in aggregate output and a high versus low share of aggregate endowment received by him. With only two securities, the financial market is incomplete and traders must trade in response to the endowment shocks they receive. We assume that the endowment shares are symmetric and persistent, and we set the parameters of the Markov chain in such a way that the traders' endowments match empirical labor income dynamics.
We solve the economy recursively, increasing the horizonTuntil such point that it no longer changes the behavior of the equilibrium.13We then simulate the equilibrium quantities on 500,000 paths for 300 periods, after which the frequency distribution of the endogenous state variable(s) is invariant. More details are provided in AppendixA.3.
article.II. Equilibrium in the Absence of Tradin:
In a first step, we analyze the equilibrium in the absence of trading fees. This case can be obtained from the general model, described in SectionI, by settingεi,tandλi,tto zero for all securitiesiand all datest. This will allow us to illustrate trading behavior in a frictionless setting and can be used to contrast the results with those for the case with trading fees.
In the model, traders use available assets to smooth their consumption over time and across states. They trade because they receive stochastic endowments while the financial market is incomplete. To a degree, they are “liquidity traders.” Specifically, part of a trader's portfolio carried over∑i=1Iθl,t−1,i×(δt,i+St,i)was chosen previously as a hedge of future endowments. In reaction to a realized endowmentel,tthat deviates from the amount that a trader has hedged, he will consume more or less but also save more or less by means of the available securities∑i=1Iθl,t,i×St,i.15How much of the “extra” endowment he consumes, and how he allocates the remainder across securities, is determined endogenously. Frictions will impede that trading motive somewhat.
Because financial markets are incomplete, individual traders' stochastic discount factors will not be equated and individual consumption growth rates are not perfectly correlated.
To be more specific, we now consider the numerical illustration introduced in SectionI.C. TableIreports, conditional on a high or lowrealizedendowment share for Trader 1, the endowment and dividend income received by the trader as well as his endogenous consumption and security trading decisions. When the trader receives a high endowment share, he has a total of 0.6153 units of the consumption good available, from which he allocates 0.5282 units to consumption and 0.0870 units to saving in the form of bonds and stocks. The case of a low endowment share is symmetric, with available income of 0.3848 units, consumption of 0.4718 units, and disinvestment of 0.0870 units to enhance consumption. The difference in the amount consumed between the two states is a reflection of the degree of consumption smoothing that the trader has been able to achieve.
The second part of TableIshows that the trading motive is straightforward:the trader who receives an endowment that exceeds the amount previously hedged uses some of his funds to consume an extra amount and always (with probability 1) buys additional shares of the stock. Knowing that his endowment shock is persistent—a positive shock today signals further positive shocks in the future—he borrows (more often than not) a modest amount through the bond to buy even more stock. Symmetric trading decisions can be observed for the case of a low share of aggregate endowment.
This simple trading pattern is also illustrated as the black, dashed line in Figure2below, which shows a single sample path of the economy. It is apparent from Panel A that for all periods in which Trader 1 receives a high share of aggregate endowment (highlighted by shaded gray), he increases his investment in the stock—shown in terms of number of shares. The corresponding trading decisions for the bond are depicted in Panel C.
article.III. Equilibrium with Trading Fees:
We now turn to the dynamic properties of equilibrium in the presence of trading fees, studying the traders' consumption and trading decisions as well as asset prices and returns.
Under standard concavity assumptions on utility functions, the maximization of7subject to9and10is a convex problem. First-order conditions of optimality (including terminal conditionsθl,T,i=0) are necessary and sufficient for the optimum to be reached. In AppendixB, we derive the system of first-order conditions (identical to system11to15below, withη=0). To obtain an equilibrium, one then usually combines the first-order conditions of both traders with the market-clearing conditions, and solves the resulting system of equations. Here, for reasons explained below, we define asequenceof η-equilibria.
Definition 1.An η-equilibrium is defined as a process for the allocation of consumption{cl,t}of both traders, a process for trading decisions{θ̂l,t,i,θ̌l,t,i}of both traders, a process for posted securities prices{St,i}, a process for state prices{φl,t}, and a process for shadow prices{Rl,t,i}that solve the following system of equations for alll,i, andt:



Definition 2.An equilibrium is the limit (if it exists) of an η-equilibrium asη→0.

In the system of equations described by11to15and3, the unknown variablesRl,t,i(defined in AppendixB) correspond, in units of consumption, to the shadow price of a “paper security” valued at a unit posted price. When Traderl's inventory of securityiis large (small) at timet, the value ofRl,t,iis smaller (greater) than 1. When the shadow price of a trader reaches the value of 1 plus the cost of buying, the trader buys, and when the shadow price reaches the value of 1 minus the cost of selling, he sells.
Forη=0, the last two equations14and15are the familiar complementary-slackness conditions of Karush (1939) as well as Kuhn and Tucker (1951). In contrast, in an η-equilibrium with η finite, the complementary slackness conditions are relaxed and traders conduct a suboptimal policy. In defining equilibrium in our economy, we found it necessary to introduce the limit of a sequence of η-equilibria because, literally speaking, atη=0, the posted prices{St,i}and the shadow prices{Rl,t,i}, at times other than transaction times, become indeterminate.18We illustrate this point in Figure1.
The figure shows the traders' demands for the stock, the aggregate demand for the stock, and the traders' demands for the bond, plotted against the posted price of the stock (on the horizontal axis) for a small, positive, but finite value of η and for trading fees of 0.5% and 3.0%.19As illustrated in Panels A and B, both traders' demands for the stock exhibit some flat regions (indicated by double-headed arrows) in which it is optimal not to trade. For low trading fees (0.5%), the traders' no-trade regions do not overlap, so that the aggregate stock demand uniquely determines the equilibrium (transaction) price (see Panel C). In contrast, for high trading fees (3.0%), there exists ajoint no-trade zone. Forη=0, this implies that the aggregate demand curve has a flat part over the domain of the joint no-trade region, thus leaving the posted stock price indeterminate. However, just before the limit, the aggregate demand curve intersects the supply at a single point, which is the equilibrium posted price (highlighted by a red circle in the magnified graph of Panel D). Panels E and F show the demand for the bond, which is flat over the same region as the stock demand. The same holds for consumption (not shown).
Also, in the system of equations described by11to15and3, the unknown variablesφl,tare the customary state prices for the state prevailing at timet. They are specific to Traderlin part because the market is incomplete and in part because of trading fees. They are, of course, different from the state pricesφl,t*that prevailed in the frictionless economy.
Because aggregate output is exogenous, with fees refunded, the probability distribution of aggregate consumption is unaffected by trading fees. But the joint distribution of the traders' individual consumptions reflects asset holdings, which are affected because trading fees create impediments to trade. That is, traders face a trade-off between the goal of smoothing consumption and the goal of smoothing holdings, with the latter being due to the desire to reduce trading fees.
Remark 1.In the presence of trading fees, traders smooth their consumption across states less effectively than they do in the frictionless economy, leading to an increase in the individual trader's consumption growth volatility. Since aggregate consumption volatility is unchanged, the increased individual consumption volatility must be matched with a reduced correlation of individual consumptions. The illustration of SectionIVhighlights these effects further (quantitatively).

The dual variablesRl,t+1,i, in addition to the marginal rates of substitutionφl,t+1, drive the prices of assets that are subject to trading fees, as do the shadow prices of the liquidity constraints in the “LAPM” of Holmström and Tirole (2001).20In effect, there are two distinct pricing kernels or state prices: one,φl,t+1, applies to the time-t+1payoffs paid in consumption units, and the other,φl,t+1×Rl,t+1,i, applies to the time-t+1posted price.

The objective is to establish a contrast with the statement of Amihud and Mendelson (1986a) that attributes the effect of trading fees on a security's price to the present value of the future-fee cash expense itself. Our proposition also makes more precise the statement of Vayanos (1998, p. 26) that “the effect of transaction costs issmallerthan the present value of transaction costs incurred by a sequence of marginal investors” (emphasis added).21Denoting all quantities in the zero-trading-fees economy with an asterisk (*), in AppendixCwe derive the following proposition.

That is, the two asset prices,St,iandSt,i*, differ by two components: (i) the current shadow priceRl,t,i, which acts as a factor that we know from Proposition1is at most as large as the one-way trading fees, and (ii) the present value of all future price differences arising from the differences in one-period marginal rates of substitution,Δφl,τ/φl,τ−1−φl,τ*/φl,τ−1*.
The differences in consumption plans highlighted in Remark1influence the future state prices and explain the differences in prices. We emphasize this result in the following remark.
Remark 2.The difference between securities prices in the absence and in the presence of trading fees is not given by the present value of anticipated trading fees.

Rather, the reason for the difference is that traders do not hold the optimal, frictionless portfolios and therefore their consumption processes differ from those that would prevail in the absence of trading fees. The increased volatility of individual consumption plays a role in setting the price because of the marginal utilities, and the reduced correlation of individual consumption also plays a role via the termΔφl,τ×(δτ,i+Sτ,i*). More details on this are provided below in relation to Observation2.
The difference between the proposition of Amihud and Mendelson (1986a) and ours is due to the fact that Amihud and Mendelson exogenously force investors to trade, whereas in our setting traders trade optimally. The difference is not to be ascribed to our assumption that the fees are refunded. If the fees had been a deadweight loss, the effect of that expense still would have been felt on consumption only—it would not have appeared directly in the present-value formula in the form of altered future cash flows on the security being priced, and Proposition2would have been equally valid.
Our solution algorithm blends in an original fashion a shift of equations; proposed by Dumas and Lyasoff (2012) to facilitate backward induction, with the Interior-Point algorithm, which is an optimization technique based on Karush (1939) and Kuhn and Tucker (1951) first-order conditions. The “time shift” of Dumas and Lyasoff (2012) involves shifting all first-order conditions, but not the kernel and market-clearing conditions, forward in time and letting traders at timetplan their time-t+1consumption but choose their time-tportfolio (which, in turn, will finance the time-t+1consumption).
The Interior-Point algorithm amounts to solving the system of equations—consisting of equations11to15and3—for vanishing values of the number η that relaxes the complementary-slackness conditions. This corresponds closely to our definition of the η-equilibria. In one very convenient implementation, Armand, Benoist, and Orban (2008) show how to add to the system of equations a single equation that drives η toward zero progressively with each step of the Newton solver. Details are provided in AppendixD.
While the Interior-Point algorithm is prominent in the area of optimization, our utilization of it in an economic equilibrium calculation is, to our knowledge, novel. Using it presents the great advantage that it allows the economist to solve a problem of equilibrium with inequality constraints in a manner exactly analogous to the one in which he solves a similar equilibrium problem without constraints or with equality constraints, by simply stacking the first-order conditions of every trader with the market-clearing condition and then solving the overall system. There is no need to implement a tatonnement on the stochastic process of prices and to implement a trader-by-trader intertemporal optimization based on the postulated process.All one needs is the relaxed Karush-Kuhn-Tucker first-order conditions. Our approach can be applied widely to cases in which the inequality constraints are such that the feasibility set remains convex: portfolio constraints, debt (also called liquidity) constraints, leverage constraints, risk constraints, etc. There are numerous instances of such constraints in the field of finance.22
article.IV. Simulation Results:
To further describe the equilibrium in the presence of trading fees, we now return to the numerical illustration introduced in SectionI.C.
First, we describe the mechanics over time of the equilibrium that we find and the transactions that take place. In the presence of trading fees, a key concept is that of a “no-trade zone,” which is the area of the state space in which both traders prefer not to trade.
Figure2displays a simulated sample path that illustrates how our financial market with trading fees operates over time, with periods of a high endowment share for Trader 1 highlighted by shaded gray, and transaction dates highlighted by a red circle. Specifically, Panels A and B depict a sample path of (i) stock holdings (expressed as a fraction of the security's supply, not as a dollar value) as they would be in a zero-trading-fee economy, (ii) actual stock holdings with a 3% trading fee, and (iii) the boundaries of the optimal no-trade zone. Note that the boundaries of the no-trade zone fluctuate in tango with the optimal frictionless holdings, except that they allow a tunnel of deviations on each side. Within that tunnel, the traders' logic is apparent: actual holdings move up or down whenever they are pushed up or down by the movement of the boundaries, with a view toward reducing the amount of trading fees paid and making sure thatas few wasteful round trips arise as possible. This leads to a trade-off between the desire to smooth consumption and the desire to smooth holdings. Panel A taken together with Panel B illustrates how wonderfully the two traders are synchronized by the algorithm: they are made to trade exactly opposite amounts at exactly the same time.
The figure also illustrates the degree to which capital is slow-moving, an issue we return to in SectionV.C. Specifically, the optimal stock holdings in the presence of trading fees are a delayed version of the frictionless holdings, but with the length of the delay being stochastic. In contrast, as shown in Panels C and D, holdings of the riskless bond, which is assumed not to entail trading fees, fluctuate more than they would in a frictionless economy. Intuitively, when traders receive their endowments, they use the cost-free, riskless bond as a holding tank and trade it much more than they would if the stock were also cost-free.
Panel E depicts the stock's posted price. While the posted price forms a stochastic process with realizations at each point in time, transaction prices materialize as a “marked point process” with realizations at random times only.23Taking Panels A, B, and E together further illustrates how the algorithm synchronizes the trades of the two traders. After an extension to more traders, the properties of this process can be confronted empirically with those of illiquid-market prices.
Even though ours is a Walrasian market and not a limit-order or a dealer market, one can define a virtual concept of bid and ask prices. In Definition3, we define the traders' private valuations of dividends. Thebid priceof a trader can then be defined as equal to the trader's private valuation of dividends divided by 1 plus the trading fee to be paid if the trader buys. Similarly, theask priceis defined as the trader's private valuation divided by 1 minus the sell fee. When the two traders' private valuations differ by the sum of the one-way trading fees, a transaction takes place. Equivalently, a trade occurs when the bid price of one trader is equal to the ask price of the other trader. This mechanism is displayed in Panel F. Defining theeffective spreadas the difference between the higher of two bid prices and the lower of the two ask prices, one could also say that a transaction takes place when the effective spread is equal to 0.
The posted price can thus be interpreted as some form of average of the two private valuations, or some form of average of the higher bid and the lower ask price (see Proposition1). In particular, we have verified quantitatively that the posted price differs very little from the bid-ask midpoint. As far as levels are concerned, the mean absolute difference between the posted price and the bid-ask midpoint divided by the effective spread is equal to 0.62 bp, 1.03 bp, and 1.52 bp for trading fees of 1%, 2%, and 3%, respectively. Also, the mean absolute difference between the rates of return on the posted price and the bid-ask midpoint are 0.00 bp, 0.01 bp, and 0.04 bp, respectively.24Hence, the empirical counterpart of any statement we make below regarding the behavior of the posted price or rates of returns on it is a testable statement involving the bid-ask midpoint, which can be viewed as a very close proxy.
Panels A and C of Figure2also illustrate that, after transitioning to a high endowment state, Trader 1 may at first not buy the stock, but buy the bond instead, buying the stock only if the high endowment persists. This happens when his prior holding is already high. When it is less high, he may buy the stock right away. To generalize the intuition provided by a single path and to give a systematic, probabilistic representation of the pattern of trading, in Figure3, we depict the transition probabilities for the “shadow price ratio”R1/(R1+R2)of the stock for the case in which Trader 1 is currently in his high endowment state and trading fees are 3%.25A shadow price ratio of 1.03 means that Trader 1 buys shares of the stock, while a ratio of 0.97 means that he sells. The figure shows the probability of a value of that ratio at timet+1conditional on a value of it at timet.26
For any value of the shadow price ratio at timet, the figure shows that the probability of a mid-level value of the ratio occurring att+1is equal to 0, or nearly so. This is a result of the trading fee being proportional—when a trader needs to trade in one direction, he trades as little as possible, knowing that he can trade repeatedly the next few times at no greater cost than he would have incurred if he had traded in a lump. The ratio therefore transitions to either a high value near the buy boundary (left-hand ridge in the diagram) if Trader 1 remains in the high-endowment state, or to a very low value at the sell boundary (right-hand ridge) if his endowment shifts to the low level.27In summary, most of the time, the equilibrium system is near a trading boundary, or, equivalently, the steady-state probability distribution of the shadow price ratio is U-shaped.28
We now discuss the average effect of trading fees on the equilibrium, illustrating first the statements that we made above in Remark1and in Propositions1and2.
In the presence of trading fees, the traders face a trade-off between smoothing consumption and smoothing holdings. We now display the endogenous consumption choices of the two traders for the numerical illustration (see Remark1).
Panels A and B of Figure4plot the average conditional volatility of individual consumption growth and their correlation, against the rate of trading fees.29In summary, after confirming that similar numerical results hold over the entire range of parameters indicated in AppendixA, we record the following observation for future reference.30
Observation 1.Trading fees increase the volatility of the consumption growth of both traders and reduce their correlation.

In Panel C of Figure4, we also show the effect of trading fees on traders' welfare, as measured by the permanent reduction in output in the frictionless economy that would lead to the same welfare as in the economy with trading fees. Higher trading fees lead to a reduction in welfare. For example, the equilibrium with trading fees of 3% is equivalent in terms of welfare to a 0.2% permanent drop in output for the frictionless economy—even though there is no loss of aggregate consumption stemming from trading fees (as would occur in the presence of deadweight costs). For comparison, Barro (2009) reports welfare gains in a model without habit formation of 0.73% to 1.65% for eliminatingallbusiness cycle risk.
In SectionIII.B, we derive analytical expressions that compare prices with and without trading fees. We now quantify the effect of the fees in our numerical illustration.
In general, trading fees on the stock have two effects on securities prices. First, as in Observation1, fees increase the traders' consumption growth volatility. Technically, when marginal utility is a convex function, any increase in the variance of individual consumption increases the expected value of future individual marginal utility and, in turn, securities prices. This effect, resulting from the trading fees, is similar to the effect of increased volatility resulting from more volatile nontradable endowment shocks (“background risk” ) in the absence of trading fees, which we might hesitantly call a precautionary-savings effect. With positive prudence, this effect encourages saving, reduces the rate of interest, and, all else equal, decreases the discount rate and increases the prices of all securities. In Figure5, Panel A, this effect explains the entire increase in the price of the short-term bond as one increases trading fees.31
Observation 2.The increase in consumption volatility that arises with trading fees drives precautionary savings higher. This reduces the interest rate, which tends to raise all securities prices. For the bond, the cash payment of which is riskless, this is the only effect. For the stock, which pays a risky dividend, this effect is only partially counteracted by another effect also driven in part by the increased consumption volatility, but in addition by the reduced positive correlation between consumption and dividends. The second effect increases the (negative) perpetual risk premium and depresses stock prices.
Next, we examine the changes in trading strategies induced by trading fees. Similar to the discussion in SectionI.C, we focus on the first trader's trading strategies in response to an endowment shock. Panels A and B of Figure6depict the (dollar) change in stock holdings as well as bond holdings (the value of the bond purchased or sold minus the redemption value of the bond having matured),33conditional on the realized endowment share for Trader 1 and normalized by aggregate output.
The intercepts of the curves, for zero trading fees, are identical to the numbers reported in TableI. For instance, in the case of a high endowment share and no trading fees, Trader 1 invests 0.1046 units of consumption in the stock and borrows 0.0176 units through the bond. As trading fees increase, the change in stock holdings is gradually reduced (Panel A). The change in bond holdings, shown in Panel B, is more striking. At zero trading fees on the stock, when receiving a high endowment, the trader borrows against future endowments to leverage his investment in the stock. However, he gradually gives up this strategy as fees rise and starts using the bond, on which there is no fee, as a “substitute.” After confirming that these numerical results hold over the entire range of parameters indicated in AppendixA, we reach the following observation.
Observation 3.There exists a level of trading fees below which the bond, which can be traded without fee, serves to enhance the investment in the stock, and above which it partially replaces the investment in the stock as a means to optimize consumption over time.

The same pricing mechanism reported in SectionIV.B.2, as it affects rates of return, is illustrated in Figure7. The figure shows the effect of trading fees on the return-generating processes. As expected, the rate of interest is reduced due to the precautionary-savings effect, matching the bond price result. In contrast, the expected stock return is left basically unchanged by trading fees. The effect on the equity premium follows. As can be seen, it increases quite markedly with trading fees. The volatility of stock returns is increased somewhat by fees—in line with the empirical findings of Hau (2006). Finally, the net effect on the Sharpe ratio is an increase. In the next section, we decompose the rates of return into premia and, returning to dynamics, show how the premia behave over time.
article.V. Applications:
We now develop three applications of our approach. First, we study the pricing of liquidity risk, in particular, the CCAPM that arises in the presence of trading fees and endogenous trading. Second, we consider an extension to three traders. Third, we study the reaction of asset prices to shocks in models with frictions.
In the equilibrium with trading fees, the CCAPM is described by equation16. It is specific to each trader; we make no attempt at aggregation across traders.34This expression can be used to show how, in the presence of trading fees, various premia arise relative to the classic CCAPM.
Letτl,t+1,idenote the change in liquidity, defined as follows.
Definition 4.(Liquidity change)


Note that1−Rl,t+1,iis a shadow trading fee rate that applies to assetiat timet+1from the point of view of Traderl, so that(1−Rl,t+1,i)×St+1,iis a future shadow dollar amount of the trading fee. Accordingly,((1−Rl,t+1,i)×St+1,i)/St,iis the drag on the asset's rate of return, created by future trading, or the “dollar cost per dollar invested” in the words of Acharya and Pedersen (2005). Current liquidity,(1−Rl,t,i)×rt+1,1, captures the fact that one dollar of the asset is potentially purchased or sold today against the riskless asset, including interest on the fee.
The first part of equation19is the CCAPM expression of a frictionless market, that is, the risk-free rate minus the covariance between an asset's return and the pricing kernel. The remainder captures a deviation from the CCAPM, which we can split into two components.
Definition 5.(Components of the CCAPM deviation)


The deviation due to the expected liquidity change20captures the fact that securityiis potentially purchased or sold tomorrow. The liquidity risk premium21, in contrast, reflects the fact that the dollar fee to be paid upon potential resale is uncertain.
Equation19gives us a decomposition similar to that performed by Acharya and Pedersen (2005). Here, however, the terms are explicitly related to theoptimal decisionof traders to trade or not trade, and all quantities have explicit and endogenous dynamics.
With these deviations from the classic CCAPM in mind, we can now study endogenous liquidity risk premia for the numerical illustration introduced in SectionI.C. Specifically, Panels A and B of Figure8depict the total CCAPM deviation as well as the two components separately, for different trading fees.
As expected, the absolute CCAPM deviation is increasing in trading fees. Amihud and Mendelson (1986a) explain that the total premium should be concave in the size of trading fees. For that reason, Amihud and Mendelson (1986b) fit the cross-section of equity portfolio returns to the log of the bid-ask spread of the previous period. They find a highly significant relationship. Our figure also exhibits that concavity property.36For trading fees of 3%, the total deviation reaches about 60 bp, which is less than the trading fees themselves, measured as a percentage of the value of each trade. Such deviation is too small to account for the several percentage points of returns that empirical researchers commonly attribute to liquidity premia.37But it does illustrate the role played by trading frictions when we try to explain empirical deviations from classic asset pricing models. Panel B reveals that the unconditional average value of the CCAPM deviation is due mostly to the liquidity risk premium (as presupposed by Pástor and Stambaugh (2003)), while the expected liquidity change is comparatively small, unconditionally speaking.38
Panel C of Figure8shows, however, that the unconditional variance of the conditionally expected liquidity term is the larger one and therefore is most responsible for the fluctuations in the deviations over time.39An important benefit of our model, in which theliquidity variable is endogenized, is the ability to study the variation of each term over time.
This theoretical contrast between the conditional and the unconditional pictures should provide guidance for empirical researchers studying illiquid markets and trying to decide which of the two terms is more important. Bongaerts, de Jong, and Driessen (2017, p. 1259), for instance, study the effect of liquidity on corporate bond expected returns and “find a strong effect of expected liquidity and equity market liquidity risk on expected corporate bond returns, while there is little evidence that corporate bond liquidity risk exposures explain expected corporate bond returns, even during the recent financial crisis.” Our model shows that, here especially, empirical conclusions could vary markedly depending on conditioning.
It might be argued that deviations from the frictionless equilibrium will be lower when more traders are in the market.40That is, if one trader wants to trade, he is more likely to find a counterparty when there are many traders. The comparative dynamics of trades between economies with two and three traders can help address this concern. This is the first reason we now study an extension to the case of three traders. The second reason is that we would like to know whether most trades would be bilateral trades—one order matching another—or whether most would be trilateral and “centralized.”
When extending the economy to three traders, we keep the exogenous process for aggregate output unchanged and continue to assume that traders are symmetric (see AppendixA.2). Because there are now three, rather than two, traders, individual endowments are no longer perfectly negatively correlated. Instead, they have an idiosyncratic component.41
Panel A of Figure9shows that, for all levels of trading fees (including zero fees), trading volume for the stock is higher in the three-trader economy than in the two-trader economy. Intuitively, with more traders in a market, while there are more people potentially ready to trade, there are also more people who need to trade (for partially idiosyncratic reasons). Naturally, as the trading fees now impact a higher volume, each trader's welfare loss due to trading fees is actually greater when three symmetric traders are present (Panel B). For example, while the two-trader equilibrium with trading fees of 3% was equivalent in welfare terms to a 0.2% permanent reduction in output for the frictionless economy, with three traders it is equivalent to a reduction of 0.35%. In summary, although trading takes place over a wider part of the state space, it is not the case that the effect of trading fees goes away as one increases the number of traders, as long as it is increased in a way that preserves symmetry between traders (with the associated increase in idiosyncratic risk).42
While it is true that each trader now faces two candidate trading counterparts, the counterparts' endowments are now less negatively correlated with his own than before. In particular, while the first effect increases the probability of stock trading, the second effect reduces it. Panel C shows that the first effect dominates and, in line with the increased trading volume, the probability of a trade in the stock market occurring increases with the introduction of a third trader. Interestingly, Panel D shows that, even though the probability ofanytrade occurring is higher in the three-trader economy, the average time each trader waits for a trade in the stock increases when going from two to three traders.
These two observations can be reconciled only if, in the three-trader setting, a sizeable fraction of trades in the stock market take place between two traders only—a bilateral trade. When three traders trade, the trade imbalance between the two largest traders is balanced by the smallest one, which might be called the “minority trade.” TableIIshows, for different trading fees, the relative frequency with which the minority trade in the stock market represents less than a given percentage of total stock volume on the simulated path. In particular, for a threshold of zero, it shows the frequency of bilateral trades, which are orders oftwotraders only that match exactly. While for trading fees of 1% one-quarter of the trades are purely bilateral, this frequency increases to 43% for trading fees of 3%. In an extension to more than three traders, these numbers could be confronted with actual market-clearing data used in the empirical microstructure literature.
Duffie (2010) gives numerous examples—with supporting empirical evidence—of situations in which investment capital does not adjust immediately but rather seems to move slowly toward profitable trades. That is, when a shock occurs, the price of a security reacts quickly, while quantities adjust more slowly. As quantities finally adjust, the price movement is reversed. Duffie's examples include additions to and deletions from the S&P 500 index, the arrival of a new order in the book, natural disasters impacting insurance markets, and the issuance of U.S. Treasury securities affecting yields, as well as many other “price-pressure” situations.43
One may think of several approaches to the modeling of slow-moving capital. Duffie (2010) himself offers a model in which the attention of traders is limited. In a similar spirit, Duffie, Gârleanu, and Pedersen (2002,2007), Duffie and Strulovici (2012), and Duffie, Malamud, and Manso (2014) assume that trading requires the physical encounter of one trader with another and that searching for encounters is costly. In the microstructure literature (which does not provide a general-equilibrium model), imperfect, noncompetitive intermediation or asymmetric information is the reason behind the slow movement of capital. Here, we offer a third, more basic microfoundation for sluggish capital. Specifically, in our model, capital moves slowly simply because the financial industry operates at a cost, which can be interpreted as a trading cost or fee. Compared to limited attention, our theory has the advantage of simplicity and absence of irrationality. It only makes use of a very basic, indeed, classic, economic principle, according to which access to a service is not free.
In Duffie (2010, p. 1249), in response to an aggregate supply shock, capital moves slowly because some traders are inattentive at the time of the shock, so that “supply or demand shocks must be absorbed […] by a limited set of investors.” Only when the inattentive traders re-enter the market is the price movement reversed. He displays the effect in the form of impulse-response functions for securities prices.44Below, we similarly evaluate the equilibrium price response to traders' endowment shocks and compare the price responses for the case of trading fees and the case of stochastic inattention.
In most published work, an impulse-response function is defined as the path followed by an endogenous variable after an exogenous shock of arbitrary size occurs at a specific time,followed by a complete absence of shocks. That is, after the shock, the exogenous variables of the economy remain at the same level as they are directly after the shock. In essence, the economy becomes deterministic. Observe that the probability of such a path is equal to 0. Therefore, it is not representative of what one would observe if the economy were treated as an ongoing entity. A different definition of an impulse-response function is called for that shows the effect of ashock occurring along the way. In AppendixE, we explain the concept of an impulse-response function that is adapted to an economy with ongoing shocks. In short, one has to compare two conditionally expected paths that depend on the shock that occurs at a given impulse time.45
To get as close to empirical work as possible, we seek to have available a transaction price at all times. For that reason, we adapt our three-trader model. First, we consider the case in which only one of the three traders (Trader 1) has to pay trading fees, while the other two do not face trading fees. Next, we consider the case in which one of the traders (Trader 1) might become (stochastically) inattentive, while the other two are always free to trade. In both settings, Traders 2 and 3 effectively trade all the time, delivering a transaction price.
The impulse responses are shown in Figure10for the two alternative impulse-response functions—with and without ongoing future shocks. By way of benchmark, the price response in a frictionless market is also shown (as the solid line) and is perfectly flat in both cases because the three traders are free to trade and are able to stabilize the price. This is true irrespective of the fact that the endowment impulse is followed by other shocks.
In contrast, in the case of 2% trading fees, the stock price is depressed by about 30 basis points relative to the frictionless price when the fee-paying trader receives a positive impulse. Intuitively, in the absence of a trading fee, he would have invested some of his endowment income in the stock. Since he does so in a smaller amount in the presence of trading fees, the average price is lower. Over time, however, theprice reverts back.
At the time of a supply or demand shock, the entire population of investors would stand ready to absorb the quantity of the asset supplied or demanded, with an excess price concession relative to a neoclassical model that is bounded by marginal trading costs. After the associated price shock, price reversals would not be required to clear the market.
It is true that, when the shock hits, all traders adjust immediately, and less so than they would in the absence of fees. Then, with the common concept of impulse-response criticized above, there is no need for further adjustment and there is no reversal, since there is no further shock after the one shock of the impulse. This is illustrated in Panel B, which shows an impulse-response function for a shock, followed by a complete absence of future shocks.
However, with our definition, which is closer to empirical work because it compares conditionally expected paths, after the impulse the effect gradually disappears, that is, there is a reversal (Panel A).46In particular, when the shock hits, all traders adjust immediately, but on an equilibrium path with ongoing shocks, traders will also react later on. This is true because of hysteresis. Indeed, the impulse has moved the fee-paying trader closer to a trade boundary, so that when later shocks arrive in the same direction, he will act more than he would have acted in the absence of the impulse. This causes the price reversal.
Consider now the case in which Trader 1 may randomly become inattentive fornperiods. The trader still optimizes his decisions intertemporally when he is attentive, and he rationally anticipates becoming inattentive.47As in Duffie (2010), one has to choose the probability of becoming inattentive and the periods of inattentionn. For the illustration in Figure10, we set the probability of the trader becoming inattentive to 0.8 and assume that he becomes inattentive for three periods. With these choices, the two economies—the one with trading fees and the one with stochastic inattention—have about the same aggregate trading volume.
Panel A displays the impulse-response function under limited attention for the case with ongoing future shocks. Here again, the reaction of the price to the impulse is immediate and approximately equal to 25 bp, and we observe a slow reversal as in Duffie (2010). Note that, for inattention, slow price reversal can also be observed in the case of a complete absence of future shocks (Panel B). Intuitively, even if there are no future shocks, the price changes because the inattentive trader re-enters the market.48
In summary, it is clear that, in the case of ongoing shocks, the time path of the trading-fee model is very similar to that of the limited-attention model. As far as responses to endowment shocks are concerned, the two are empirically indistinguishable.
article.VI. Conclusion:
In this paper, we develop a general-equilibrium model of a financial market with trading fees. We describe its properties, that is, trading strategies, asset prices, and returns. We further define a concept of Walrasian equilibrium for this market and invent an algorithm that delivers an exact numerical equilibrium. The algorithm synchronizes the traders in the implementation of their trades and allows us to analyze how trades take place and prices are formed and evolve.
We analytically compare the equilibrium securities prices in the presence of trading fees to those without trading fees as well as to the traders' private valuations, and explain how the gap between them triggers trades. Using a numerical setting with two traded securities—a bond and a stock—we quantitatively study the impact of trading fees. We find that the trade-off between smoothing consumption and smoothing holdings leads to a higher volatility of individual consumption, a lower correlation between individual consumptions, and a reduction in welfare. Securities prices are actually increased by the presence of fees. The price increase of the bond is related to the increased volatility of individual consumption growth, which decreases the rate of interest in the manner of precautionary savings. For the stock, this effect is only partially offset by a perpetual-risk discount that increases with individual consumption volatility. As for rates of return, we show that the equity premium, stock return volatility, and Sharpe ratio of equity are increased.
Finally, we develop three applications of our model. First, we endogenously obtain the behavior over time of the various components of a CCAPM that incorporates trading fees. We identify the risk factors as well as their relative sizes and movements over time. Second, we study an extension of our model to the case of three traders. We show that most trades are bilateral and that the impact of trading fees does not decrease with the introduction of more (symmetric) traders. Third, we compare the impulse responses of this model to those of a model in which trading is infrequent because of trader inattention. Contrary to what has been asserted, limited-attention models and trading-fees models produce very similar price responses if one considers the case of an economy with ongoing future shocks. Thus, so far, they are equally good contenders as models of slow-moving capital.
The model generates a rich set of predictions that are empirically refutable because we verify quantitatively that the bid-ask midpoint can be used as a proxy to study its predictions. For example, similar to our results, many empirical papers find a “liquidity premium” for less liquid stocks, though a quantitatively bigger one. The theoretical nature of our paper allows one to evaluate the adequacy of extant empirical tests of CCAPMs that include a premium for liquidity risk, and thus should guide future empirical work. By empirically studying the price reversal for stocks with different levels of liquidity, one should also be able to shed more light on the underlying explanation for price reversal. In particular, our model predicts that, for a more liquid stock, the price reverts back at a faster rate.
Viewing trading fees as a metaphor for the cost (plus markup) of keeping the financial sector in operation, our paper improves our understanding of the effect of this cost on financial-market equilibrium.
Future work should aim to model an equilibrium in which trading would not be Walrasian. In such a model, the rate of trading fees would not be given and traders would submit limit and market orders. The behavior of the limit-order book would thus be obtained. This work would be similar to Parlour (1998), Foucault (1999), Foucault, Kadan, and Kandel (2005), Goettler, Parlour, and Rajan (2005), and Roşu (2009), except that trades would arrive at the time and in quantities of the traders' choice, and would not be driven by an exogenous process.49

article.Appendix A: Numerical Illustrations:
In this appendix, we describe the settings that we use to illustrate the dynamics of equilibrium in the presence of trading fees. They are meant only to illustrate, in a stylized fashion, the workings of the model. They cannot be seen as being calibrated to a real-world economy because we have two (three) traders, not millions; two securities, not tens of thousands; and a trading frequency of one year. Thus, although we incorporate a motive to trade that is present at all times, the volume of trading does not come anywhere close to market data.
There exist two securities:i=1, a short-lived riskless security (the “bond”) in zero net supply that is not subject to trading fees, andi=2, a long-lived claim (the “stock” ) for which trading entails trading fees. The stock is in unit net supply and pays out dividendδt,2, which is modeled as a constant fraction χ of aggregate output.
The remainder, total output minus dividend,(1−χ)×Ot, is distributed as endowments,el,t, to the two traders. In particular, we assume that the fractions of aggregate endowment,υl,t, follow a simple symmetric two-state Markov chain, with realizations of 62.5% (ranging from 55% to 70%) and 37.5%. We set the probabilitypof transitioning from a high (low) state today to a high (low) state in the next period to a central value of 0.85 (ranging from 0.75 to 0.9). The central values of these parameters imply a volatility of 20% for the endowment shocks, which is comparable to the volatility of labor income shocks of about 24% (see Gourinchas and Parker (2002)).
Finally, we choose β,h, γ, and χ to match the empirical moments of the risk-free rate, the equity risk premium, stock market volatility, and the wealth-income ratio. The resulting central values areβ=0.98(range: [0.96 to 0.99]),h=0.2(range: [0.1 to 0.3]),γ=7.5(range: [2.5 to 10]), andχ=0.15(range: [0.05 to 0.25]). With 85% of total output being distributed as endowments, the average wealth-income ratio in our economy is 3.81, comparable to the ratios documented by the 2014 Survey of Consumer Finances of 0.37 (age <35) to 5.52 (age ≥75). The return moments resulting from the central values are reported in TableAI, which demonstrates that our quantitative experiments are conducted in a realistic financial-market setting.
For both three-trader economies, we keep the surplus consumption ratio unchanged, that is, we seth=0.1333and each trader gets, on average, one-third of aggregate endowment. The economy in SectionV.Bis a simple extension of the two-trader economy presented above. We again model a symmetric Markov chain, now with three states to accommodate the third trader. The endowment share realizations are 45%, 27.5%, and 27.5%. The probability of one trader transitioning from the high endowment share today to the high endowment share in the next period is set to 0.7, and the probability of transitioning to a state in which one of the other two traders receives a high endowment share is 0.15 each.
In SectionV.C, we preserveconditionalsymmetry between Traders 1 and 2. That is, we consider a compound of two 2 × 2 Markov chains for endowments. While in the first chain Trader 3 gets a share of either 41.68% or 24.98%, in the second chain the remainder is distributed to the other two traders, with each getting either 39% or 61%. Both separate Markov chains are persistent, with the probability of staying in a state being 0.85.

Definition A.1.A steady state of our economy is a combination of the joint probability distributions of all variables at timestandt+1such that two conditions are met:



In all of our numerical illustrations, we run the algorithm backward from a fixed horizon date until the first condition is met, that is, until there is no change in any of the functions being carried backward, obtaining an equilibrium where traders are very long-lived.50
Besides displaying features that hold for a very long horizon, we also want to make sure that those features do not depend on initial conditions. For that purpose, we simulate the long-horizon economy forward and we keep track of the frequency distribution of the state variable(s) across simulated paths. We stop the simulations when the distribution of the state variable(s) has converged. That is the second condition. For all results reported in the paper, we only employ datestandt+1from this “steady state era.” FigureA1illustrates this for the economy with two traders in the absence of trading fees. The steady-state probability distribution of consumption shares obtains after about 250 periods, after which the distribution does not change any longer. Thus, 250 periods is a long enough “burn-in” history. In this paper, our results are based on quantities att=300.

Note that, from a computational standpoint, the shift of equations amounts to letting traders at timetplan their time-t+1consumptioncl,t+1,jbut choose their time-tportfolioθl,t,i(which, in turn, will finance the time-t+1consumption).
The above system of equations comprises2Kt+2Kt+I+2KtI+2KtI+2KtI+Iequations (not counting the inequalities) with2Kt+2Kt+2KtI+2I+2KtI+2Kt+Iunknowns{cl,t+1,j,φl,t+1,j,Rl,t+1,i,j,θl,t,i,θ̂l,t+1,i,j,θ̌l,t+1,i,j;l=1,2;i=1,…,I;j=1,…,Kt}.51
Besides the exogenous endowmentsel,t+1,jand dividendsδt+1,i,j, the “givens” are the time-ttrader-specific shadow prices of both consumption{φl,t;l=1,2}and paper securities{Rl,t,i;l=1,2;i=1,…,I}, which must henceforth be treated as state variables and which we refer to as “endogenous state variables.” Given the nature of the equations, the latter variables can be reduced to state variablesR2,t,iR1,t,iandφ1,tφ1,t+φ2,t, all of which are naturally bounded a priori:1−εi,t1+λi,t≤R2,t,iR1,t,i≤1+λi,t1−εi,tand0≤φ1,tφ1,t+φ2,t≤1.52
The given securities' price functionsSt+1,i,jand the given future position functionsθl,t+1,i,jare obtained by backward induction of the previous solution of the system. All functions carried backward are interpolated by means of quadratic interpolation based on the modified Shepard method.
Moving back through time untilt=0, the last portfolio holdings calculated are the post-trade (exiting) portfolios at time 0:θl,0,i. To meet the initial conditionsθ¯l,i, we translate these into entering (pre-trade) holdings. This is done by solving a system of equations that consists of the traders' budget constraints, definitions, complementary-slackness conditions, and market-clearing conditions for time-0, but not the kernel conditions, which have already been solved.53
The system of equations described above can be solved numerically by Newton iterations. However, the iterations can run into indeterminacy because of the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker complementary-slackness conditions (D1andD2), which contain a product of unknowns equal to 0. Indeed, if a Newton step produces, for instance, a value−Rl,t+1,i,j+1+λi,t+1,jon the boundary, where it is equal to 0, then the requirement placed onθ̂l,t+1,i,j−θl,t,idrops out of the system and indeterminacy follows. The Interior-Point algorithm is a solution to this problem. It amounts to replacing the above system of equations by a sequence of equation systems in which the complementary-slackness conditions are relaxed, as shown in equations14and15where η is a small number that is made to approach zero as the algorithm progresses. In this way, indeterminacy is avoided.
article.Appendix E: Impulse-Response Functions:
A new definition of an impulse-response function is called for to reflect the concept of a shock that occurs along the way.
We generate 500,000 paths, at each point in time drawing three [0, 1] uniform random numbers—and transforming them through cumulative probability distributions as needed—to determine (i) whether total output goes up or down, (ii) whether Trader 3 gets a high or a low share (first Markov chain), and (iii) whether Trader 1 or Trader 2 gets a high or a low endowment share (second Markov chain). Importantly, note that the draws from the uniform distribution, as opposed to the output and endowment realizations themselves, make uppurely transient processes.
Next, we segregate the paths into two subsets depending on whether the third draw from the uniform distribution (setting the endowment share between Traders 1 and 2) is above or below 0.5—“the impulse.” We compute the average of each of these two sets of paths and take the difference between them. This is the difference between two sets of paths, both of which are expected conditional on two levels of the impulse. They represent the effect of the impulse that one would observe on average.
